In al-Ma'ani, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of His saying: اهدنا الصراط المستقيم (Guide us on the straight path): يعني ارشدنا الى لزوم الطريق المؤدي الى محبتك ، والمبلغ الى جنتك ، والمانع من أن نتبع اهواءنا فنعطب ، او ان نأخذ بارائنا فنهلك — "It means: direct us to adhere to the road that leads to Your love, conveys to Your Paradise, and prevents us from following our desires so that we perish, or taking our own opinions so that we are destroyed."

Also in al-Ma'ani, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: يعني ، ادم لنا توفيقك الذي اطعناك به في ماضي ايامنا ، حتى نطيعك كذلك في مستقبل اعمارنا — "It means: continue for us Your success (توفيقك) by which we obeyed You in our past days, so that we may obey You likewise in the future of our lives."

I say: The two narrations represent two different ways of answering the doubt regarding the necessity of acquiring what is already possessed by seeking guidance for the one already guided. The first narration looks at the variation in the levels of guidance in their external reality (مصداقاً), while the second looks at their unity in conceptual meaning (مفهوماً).

Also in al-Ma'ani, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him): الصراط المستقيم في الدنيا ما قصر عن الغلو ، وارتفع عن التقصير واستقام ، وفي الآخرة طريق المؤمنين الى الجنة — "The straight path in this world is that which falls short of extremism (الغلو), rises above negligence (التقصير), and remains upright; and in the Hereafter, it is the road of the believers to Paradise."

Also in al-Ma'ani, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of "the path of those..." (صراط الذين): اي : قولوا : اهدنا صراط الذين أنعمت عليهم بالتوفيق لدينك وطاعتك ، لا بالمال والصحة ، فانهم قد يكونون كفاراً او فساقاً ، قال : وهم الذين قال الله : « وَمَن يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولَـٰئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَدَاءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولَـٰئِكَ رَفِيقًا » — "That is: say: 'Guide us to the path of those whom You have blessed with success (التوفيق) for Your religion and Your obedience, not with wealth and health, for they might be disbelievers or transgressors.' He said: 'And they are those of whom Allah said: "Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!" (4:69).'"

In al-Uyun, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him), from his fathers, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) say: 'Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, said: "I have divided the Opening of the Book (فاتحة الكتاب) between Myself and My servant; half of it is for Me and half of it is for My servant, and My servant shall have what he asks for." When the servant says: بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the Most Merciful), Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, says: "My servant has begun with My name, and it is incumbent upon Me to complete his affairs for him and bless him in his states." When he says: الحمد لله رب العالمين (All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds), Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, says: "My servant has praised Me and recognized that the blessings he has are from Me and that the afflictions diverted from him are by My grace; I call you to witness that I shall add the blessings of the Hereafter to his blessings in this world and divert from him the afflictions of the Hereafter as I diverted from him the afflictions of this world." When he says: الرحمن الرحيم (the All-beneficent, the Most Merciful), Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, says: "My servant has testified for Me that I am the All-beneficent, the Most Merciful; I call you to witness that I shall surely provide him an ample portion of My mercy and a generous share of My gift." When he says: مالك يوم الدين (Master of the Day of Retribution), Allah the Exalted says: "I call you to witness that just as he acknowledged that I am the Master of the Day of Retribution, I shall surely make his reckoning easy on the Day of Account, accept his good deeds, and overlook his evil deeds." When he says: إياك نعبد (You [alone] do we worship), Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, says: "My servant has spoken the truth; Me [alone] does he worship. I call you to witness that I shall surely reward him for his worship with a reward that everyone who opposed him in his worship of Me will envy." When he says: وإياك نستعين (and to You [alone] do we look for help), Allah the Exalted says: "From Me has My servant sought help and to Me has he fled for refuge; I call you to witness that I shall surely help him in his affair, aid him in his hardships, and take his hand on the day of his calamities." When he says: إهدنا الصراط المستقيم (Guide us on the straight path) to the end of the Surah, Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, says: "This is for My servant, and My servant shall have what he asks for; I have answered My servant, given him what he hoped for, and secured him from that which he feared."'"

I say: Al-Saduq narrated something similar in al-Ilal from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him). The narration, as you see, explains Surah al-Fatiha within the prayer (الصلوة). It supports what has been mentioned repeatedly: that the Surah is a speech belonging to Him, the Glorified, acting as a proxy for His servant in what he mentions in the station of worship and the manifestation of servitude—from the praise of his Lord to the manifestation of his worship. It is a Surah designed for worship, and there is no Surah in the Quran that parallels it in its function. By that, I mean:

First: The Surah in its entirety is a speech spoken by Allah, the Glorified, in the station of proxy for His servant in what he says when he turns his face toward the station of Lordship and places himself in the station of servitude.

Second: It is divided into two parts: half for Allah and half for the servant.

Third: It contains all the Quranic knowledges despite its brevity and conciseness. For the Quran, in its wondrous breadth of fundamental knowledges and the branches that stem from them—including ethics, rulings on worship, transactions, politics, social matters, promises, threats, stories, and lessons—returns in its collective explanations to monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood (النبوة), and the Resurrection (المعاد) and their branches, and to the guidance of servants to what rectifies their worldly and otherworldly affairs. This Surah, as is clear, contains all of these in the briefest wording and clearest meaning.

You should compare what is manifest to you of the beauty of this Surah, which Allah the Glorified has placed in the prayer of the Muslims, with what the Christians place in their prayer from the speech found in the Gospel of Matthew (6:9-13), which we mention in its Arabic wording: "Our Father who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one. Amen."

Reflect on the meanings conveyed by the words of these sentences as "heavenly knowledges" and what they contain of the etiquette of servitude. It mentions first that their father (who is Allah, hallowed be His name) is in the heavens!! Then it supplicates for the Father that His name be hallowed, His kingdom come, and His will be carried out on earth as it is in heaven—but who is to answer this supplication, which resembles the slogans of political parties? Then it asks Allah to give the bread of the day and to meet forgiveness with forgiveness, making the overlooking of a right contingent upon their own overlooking. And what right would they have if Allah had not granted them a right? It asks Him not to test them but to save them from the evil one—and that is impossible, for this world is the abode of testing and perfection; what is the meaning of salvation without trial and testing? Then, let your wonder be complete at what was mentioned by some Orientalists among the scholars of the West, followed by some who affiliate themselves [with Islam]: that Islam does not surpass others in knowledges, for all the Sharias of Allah call to monotheism and the purification of souls through virtuous character and righteous deeds, and religions only differ in the deep-rootedness of their social fruits!!

(Another Narrative Discussion)

In al-Faqih and the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: الصراط المستقيم أمير المؤمنين عليه‌السلام — "The straight path (الصراط المستقيم) is the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him)."

In al-Ma‘ani, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), he said: "It is the path toward the gnosis (معرفة) of Allah. There are two paths: a path in this world and a path in the Hereafter. As for the path in this world, it is the Imam whose obedience is obligatory (المفترض الطاعة). Whoever recognizes him in this world and follows his guidance shall pass over the path that is the bridge over Hell in the Hereafter; but whoever does not recognize him in this world, his foot shall slip in the Hereafter and he will tumble into the fire of Hell."

Also in al-Ma‘ani, narrated from Imam al-Sajjad (عليه السلام), he said: "There is no veil between Allah and His Proof (حجته), nor is there any screen for Allah excluding His Proof. We are the gates of Allah, we are the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), we are the receptacle of His knowledge (عيبة علمه), we are the interpreters of His revelation (تراجمة وحيه), we are the pillars of His monotheism (أركان توحيده), and we are the location of His secret."

Narrated by Ibn Shahrashub from the Tafsir of Waki‘ ibn al-Jarrah, from al-Thawri, from al-Suddi, from Asbat and Mujahid, from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding the saying of the Exalted: اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ "Guide us on the straight path," he said: "Say, O assembly of servants: 'Guide us toward the love of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام).'"

I say: There are other narrations conveying these same meanings. These reports belong to the category of "flow" (الجري) — the principle of applying a Quranic verse to its ongoing instances and extensions — and the identification of the foremost referent (المصداق) of the verse. Know that "flow" (الجري) — a term we shall frequently employ in this book — is a technical term derived from the speech of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام).

(1) The virtuous priest Gustave Le Bon in The Civilization of the Arabs.

In the Tafsir of al-‘Ayyashi, narrated from Fudayl ibn Yasar, who said: I asked Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) about this narration: "There is no verse in the Quran but that it has an outer aspect (ظهر) and an inner aspect (بطن), and there is no letter in it but that it has a limit (حد), and for every limit there is a point of ascent (مُطَّلَع)." I asked: "What is meant by its outer and inner aspects?" He (عليه السلام) replied: "Its outer aspect is its revelation (تنزيله) and its inner aspect is its esoteric interpretation (تأويله). Some of it has already passed, and some of it has not yet come to be. It flows (يجري) just as the sun and the moon flow; whenever something of it arrives, the discourse occurs."

There are other narrations with this same meaning. This is the methodology (سليقة) of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام); they apply a verse of the Quran to any instance (الموارد) that is capable of being its referent, even if it lies outside the specific occasion of revelation (مورد النزول). Rational consideration (الاعتبار) supports this, for the Quran was sent down as guidance for all the worlds (العالمين), guiding them toward the necessary belief (واجب الاعتقاد), the necessary character (واجب الخلق), and the necessary action (واجب العمل). The theoretical knowledge (المعارف النظرية) it clarifies consists of realities (حقائق) that are not restricted to one state over another, nor one time over another. Similarly, any virtue or vice it mentions, or any practical ruling (حكم عملي) it legislates, is not confined to one individual over another or one era over another, due to the universality of legislation (عموم التشريع).

Furthermore, the reported occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) — the specific event or circumstance that prompted the descent of a verse or verses regarding a person or incident — does not necessitate restricting the ruling to that specific incident such that the ruling expires when the incident concludes or dies with the death of the person involved. This is because the exposition is general (عام) and the underlying reason (التعليل) is absolute (مطلق). Indeed, the praise revealed regarding certain believers, or the condemnation revealed regarding others based on the presence of specific traits in them, cannot be restricted to the person who was the occasion of revelation when those very same traits exist in another people after them, and so on. The Quran also indicates this; the Exalted says: يَهْدِي بِهِ اللَّهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوَانَهُ "With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure" (al-Ma'idah: 16). And He says: وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ لَّا يَأْتِيهِ الْبَاطِلُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ "Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it" (Fussilat: 42). And the Exalted says: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحَافِظُونَ "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it" (al-Hijr: 9).

The narrations concerning the application of Quranic verses to them (the Imams, عليه السلام) or to their enemies — meaning the narrations of "flow" (الجري) — are numerous across various chapters, perhaps reaching into the hundreds. Following this general notification, we shall omit the citation of most of them in the discussions on narrations (الأبحاث الروائية), as they fall outside the primary objective of this book, except where a specific purpose in the discussion requires it; so let this be remembered.
